what_it_does: Defines a bootstrapped decision record for whether the company should receive its own revocable write key to the shared design library so finished designs can be saved back automatically, or remain read-only for now.
observations: The file is a BOOTSTRAP decision — the first real instance of the decision-surface resolver.
It declares a single module-level DECISION dict and a long explanatory docstring; there is no executable logic.
The decision id is required to equal the file stem: merge-sa-authorize.
Decision state is intentionally not stored in the file; it resolves from the latest decision_take mark on the canonical decision:// address.
The recommended option is to give the company save-back access.
explanation_source points to board://item-c0a2d591 for the rationale.
The legibility block provides a human-readable name, a reversibility note, and a grounded 'why' drawn from recollection.
Wording is kept at operator altitude — 'design library' and 'save-back' rather than service-account or RLS details.
standouts: Decision state is externalized to a decision_take mark, so the file alone never shows pending/resolved status.
A recommended option is embedded directly in the static decision definition.
The legibility layer separates human-facing explanation from machine-facing schema fields.
A C5 grounded-explain comment explicitly distinguishes recollection from live-generated rationale.
conventions: Data-as-code pattern: a Python file containing a single dict literal.
File stem matches the decision id.
Decision schema uses id, meaning, options, scope, subtype, explanation_source, and legibility keys.
Operator-level language in meaning and implications; machine identifiers only appear in explanation_source and comments.
External references (board item, canonical address) are used instead of embedding state or rationale.
concerns: The recommended flag is static; changing the recommendation requires editing this file.
Current decision state is not inspectable from the file alone because it lives in external marks.
The board item id and canonical address are hardcoded without local validation or retrieval mechanism.
The legibility 'why' is lengthy and mixes historical context with the option description.
notes: This file bootstraps the decision surface by declaring a reversible authorization choice about design-library write access, while the actual resolved state is tracked elsewhere via decision_take marks.
questions: Where are decision_take marks stored and how is the 'latest' mark resolved?
What is the content of board://item-c0a2d591 and what criteria will drive the choice?
Which component consumes this DECISION dict and registers it with the decision:// resolver?
How is 'its own entries' enforced for the proposed write key?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to define a bootstrapped decision record for save-back authorization; the file does exactly that by declaring the decision dict and explaining that its resolved state is external.
apparent_themes: Grounded explanations tied to recollection or board sources
intention_signals: It appears to be establishing a minimal, reusable pattern for representing pending operator-level authorization decisions in code, keeping the decision definition, human-readable rationale, and runtime state separate.
novelty: A decision is modeled as a static Python data structure with a dedicated legibility layer for humans, while its resolved state lives outside the file as a decision_take mark on a canonical address.
contribution: Provides a concrete decision-record schema and a bootstrap example for save-back authorization, showing how to separate decision definition, human-readable rationale, and resolved state.
summary_for_embedding: A Python decision substrate file that defines the global merge-sa-authorize decision about granting the company a revocable write key to the shared design library versus keeping it read-only.
intention_for_embedding: To create a bootstrapped, operator-readable decision record whose resolved state is tracked by external decision_take marks rather than the file itself.
